item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta say, it was really good for its time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was viewing random TheVolgun videos at work earlier and this one was one of them that popped up. Thought I would stop by and say this is beautifully executed to the point that I teared up near the end.. very rare for that to happen to me of all people so I say well done Author/s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this scp! The power of being able to create a world so immersive is fascinating. It also reminds me slightly of the Mirror of Erised from Harry Potter, in that both of these items can disillusion people from reality.
I would like to know more about the psychological influences on a dreamer brought back to reality against their will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The pages are blank when you open it. All the pages are blank. It will write "A hero is born" on wherever page you reached first. Lets say you open it by page 30…well, it will say "A hero is born" on that page, but any other page would be blank…

I would like to know if you can have multiple people reading the book. Would it create multiple dreams? Will those adventures schedule?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“A hero is born” on the first page viewed, while all other pages will be blank, "resetting" once the book is closed.

Then the test with the camera also sees each page being blank.

When are pages not blank? What is being reset? It's a book with writing on the first page only. No changes to content are mentioned so what is resetting and why is the camera test surprising?

Also, "protagonist of a troubled land" doesn't sound right. Lands don't have protagonists, stories do. Lands could have heroes? Change protagonist to "hero figure?" Sone other rephrasing?

item.title: YES
item.content:encoded: I need this in my life. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: then it would've broken ma heart :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to see what happens if you put 423 in this book. Could someone using it meet Fred in person?

Also; really want this for myself…

item.title: Interesting
item.content:encoded: What an interesting narrative for a class "safe" scp. I do wish that the effect was not nearly as depressing as it became.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely love this SCP. I see all these old comments about anomalous storage, but it's clear that it's sentient but safe.

This is easily one of my favorite safe-class SCP's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the article and don't think it should be changed, but if I were a research member of the Foundation, I would ask:

  • What if two people read the book simultaneously? Do they share a dream, or are at least in the same fiction universe? If the latter, do they coexist in said universe?
  • What possible threats could the book pose? Can it cause any harm beyond psychological?
  • Does this book pose a security threat? What if a high-ranked Foundation member gives away classified information in the dream, and the next person to read the book becomes aware of this information? Should safety measures be taken to prevent this?
  • Can the book be used for psychological relief of Foundation members?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please do.

item.title: Review
item.content:encoded: Jesus Christ that was great. The whole thing is unique and generally lighthearted at first, the then just smacks you in the face with that reasercher committing suicide. It also does a great job making the book keeper a likeable character, further amplifying the death of the reasercher. It’s kind of sad that the death of one guy in this SCP is more impactful then the death of dozens in others.

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN SqueakyT
item.content:encoded: Necroposting is a bit vaguely defined at the moment, but historically we consider necroposting to essentially be when people respond to very old discussion threads or lines of discussion within a page but without actually advancing the discussion. Examples of necroposting are like Dr Santi's posts above - they are considered necroposting because although he is responding to criticisms, those criticisms were posted so long ago that the people he is responding to will likely never see them. He is effectively not advancing the discussion because that discussion was made six years ago. It is rather like going to a book club the day after the meeting and trying to talk - you can do it, but nobody is there to listen to you and you're just making noise.

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN SqueakyT
item.content:encoded:
Dr Magnus, I'm probably doing this all wrong. I have very little experience with computers. I'm old.
It's currently 2018, this story is still here. Like every story ever written it will always be.
So why is "necroposting" frowned upon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP. Its in my own personal top 10 favorites. Its much more unique and different from alot of the other ones. Its concept is fun and it's one of those SCP's you wish were real.

I see a lot of people complain how is containment is to light, but I feel it's fine. The Book Keeper is cooperative and has shown to not be a threat. Its in a separate area and is monitored, you need lvl 2 authorization to even get to it, you need to be evaluated in order to read it, and evaluated again after, and have to write what happened within 48 hours. There ARE things in place. It has more strict rules than 999 does. Even if things did go bad, they could always lock it up and prevent personel from reading it.

Overall, I think it's a great SCP. Have an upvote! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… I'm not crying, I swear…
Gods, this was beautifully heartbreaking. 1230-1 just wanted to give people the best dreams they've ever had. It makes me curious, though. How did the Book Keeper become what he is? Was he once a person? Suddenly I want to write a tale about him..

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded: Wikidot doesn't have an automatic notifications system, but I think you can set up an RSS feed? I'm not sure how to do that though. You'll need to ask someone who already has it set up how they did it.

item.title: Re: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded: Speaking of replies, is there a way to see when someone replies to a comment I made, aside from remembering to check back in whatever thread periodically?

item.title: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
Doctor Santi you're replying to six year old comments.

Please don't necropost, no one will reply to you, most likely.

item.title: Reasons
item.content:encoded: To dangerous. Mental harm and strain is more dangerous than anything, because you cannot contain it.

item.title: Reasons
item.content:encoded: I don't think that they let anyone play with it after Professor B[REDACTED] killed himself. The people who are allowed to read it go through extensive measures for psychiatric evaluation, in case of another event like this.

item.title: Reasons
item.content:encoded: I think it is because it s a Safe class. It is just a book. It does not need lengthy containment, and the only reason why that Professor B[REDACTED] killed himself was because he never wanted to leave, and never wanted to return to the real world. As long as you don't sleep and stay within SCP-1230-1's world and imagination to long, it is Safe, both containment and threat level. SCP-1230-1 cannot control humans, just controls his own world. And if you read the experiment logs, SCP-1230-1 has no intent of killing anyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never have I read a skip I wanted to be real more than this one.

I'd like to see some exploration of this as a means of battling depression. On the one hand, it could, possibly, be able to cure such a condition. But, on the other hand, it could well prove to be the most dangerous thing a depressed person could possibly get their hands on.

item.title: One of the few SCPs to get a visceral reaction out of me
item.content:encoded: I feel like this may be one of the few SCPs on this site that I feel is just simply perfect. It says everything it needs to say during the short time we have with it. The bookkeeper has a tremendous amount of character for a short story, and I love the ambiguity of the advice the doctor gave him at the end. Fantastic article. +1.

item.title: One of my favorites.
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept, and the Book Keeper seems very innocent and friendly by nature.

If I had anything to contribute, I would like to see some more detailed tests on SCP-1230. Basically answers to some questions one can come up with such as:

Case study of a subject with confirmed psychopathy exposed to SCP-1230
Attempts to expose other SCPs confirmed to be capable of imagination to SCP-1230
The variety of settings SCP-1230 can create

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A gamemaster that runs tabletops for you in your dreams, huh? Another skip added to my "Desperately wish this was real" list.

As for critique, the article itself is fantastic. Quite a character driven skip, it succeeds at making me empathize with the bookkeeper and the doctor. The effect is unique, and the proportional response towards a benign object is rather refreshing - I'm glad you didn't turn it into a sad tale where the keeper gets relegated to some forgotten locker forever.

item.title: Mod Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Flareblood, these kinds of posts violate the following Site Rules:

Arguments and Maturity: You may question the actions of other users and staff as long as it is done in a calm, mature fashion.

Comments: You can comment on any entry provided you are respectful to other users. All comments must follow the Criticism Policy. No personal attacks or trolling.

This is an official warning. Please refrain from making similar posts in the future. Further infractions will result in a ban from the site.

Do not reply if you are not a staff member. This is a closed staff post.

item.title: Re: Stop hating on this amazing article
item.content:encoded:
Apparently 471 more people like this than dislike it.

A lot of the comments are very positive as well.

But that said, people like different things. People will see cliches and reuses of tropes from other existing works in different ways. Some people like to see how a possible cliche is handled, some will dismiss it just for being a possible cliche.

Some people just don't like "Safe" classed items that can be locked up and forgotten about, and take against articles just for the classification.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
This is not helpful feedback. This is also not the place to vent frustrations on some negative crit against an article that's +400. People are allowed to dislike whatever they want without even needing to explain themselves.

This is a closed staff post. Do not respond if you are not staff.

item.title: Re: Stop hating on this amazing article
item.content:encoded:
1): I have never seen a comment on this page that was "whiny" and were just voicing why they disliked the article. But most of the comments about it were pretty positive, in my opinion.

2): Why does it matter to you that someone else hates/dislikes it. It does not affect you in any way, shape or form.

3):

Please people, just stop it.

Stop what? Disliking an article you like?

item.title: Stop hating on this amazing article
item.content:encoded:
Please people, just stop it.

I absolutely love this. I was hoping to see other people who did as well, but nope.

Instead, I get whiny bastards going "WAAA ITS NOT CONTAIN-Y ENOUGH" or "WAAAA ITS TOO GENERIC AND CLICHE".

Just, all of you, fuck off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the only SCP's I've read so far that I would kill to own, even SCP-662 doesn't come to close to how much I want this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved it, has a new feeling..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love to know if this can be used multiple times.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
+ Show
- Hide

I'm not trying to argue the revert, but I'd just like to explain my reasoning for making the edit, and to point out that I did make an online search before making the edit (I always double-check before making an edit). Here is what I found here:

"Onto" is a preposition that means, on top of, to a position on, upon. Onto implies movement, so it has an adverbial flavor to it even though it is a preposition. For example:

The cat jumped onto the dresser.
The gymnast jumped onto the mat.

Use "on to", two words, when on is part of a verb phrase. In instances when "on" is part of the verb, it is acting as an adverb and "to" is the preposition, which takes an object. For example,

I will log on to the computer.
She held on to the chains while swinging.

Seeing as this, as well as a few other sites I found, appeared to confirm what I remembered from my own studies, I made the edit.

Of course that doesn't mean I can't be wrong here. After making another online search I was able to find websites that claim that both "hold on to" and "hold onto" are acceptable (although I'm somewhat wary of the fact that none of them really backed their claims with detailed explanations or examples). So yeah, if you say it's a bad edit, I accept that - the grammar issue in question was certainly a minor one, regardless of whether the edit was justified. I'll try to be less nit-picky about grammar in the future.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it was unnecessary. Within the sentence:

I did my best but I could only hold onto him for 200 years.

The word "onto" has been used in a valid manner. While "on to" is also correct, it is important to respect author intention, and given that both forms are correct, there's no reason to change the author's original text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought "fantasy setting" in this case is more like "fictional" than "sword and sorcery" and is tailored to your preferences. Poor Doctor B. was definitely a D&D guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that answer is "because why should we?". There is no need for rewarding D-class, they are getting brainwashed every month either way. But I can see how 1230 could be reward for safe, cooperating human subjects. (like SCP 507)

(yes, in my headcanon D-class are treated with amnestics, not terminated. Destroying perfectly useable tool isn't very practical)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you've got an idea for test that involves a more modern-style setting, write it up as a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would this SCP be capable of creating a more modern setting that's not close to the fantasy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice. So he can adapt. I wonder if one night she'll dream of a child prince bravely questing to save her from a mean dragon-boy who kidnapped her because the King was stingy with that year's offering of candies, and plans to horribly torment her for as long as the King delays the offering.

The torment in question being incessant bouts of tickling. Or putting frogs on the princess's pretty head and watch her scream in disgust.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Book Keeper would accomodate. He is a sapient being, after all.

Take a look at Learning, in the Bellerverse Canon(can't link on mobile). It features 1230 and its effects on a young girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. My heart goes out to this scip. I hope he doesn't come across another such case (point to the Foundation for mandating psych-evals before and after using the scip).

One question, though… What would happen if the dreamer was a genuine hero, with all sorts of feats and achievements that is the stuff of legends?

And what if what the dreamer's most cherished fantasies revolved not around such heroic adventures, but rather around a world of peace and prosperity where there is no need for great heroes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, this is one of my favorite scips.That said, there is one part that I wonder about:

D-class requested permission to "give it another go". Request was denied.

Why was he denied? As many say, the Foundation is "cold, not cruel", & since this book can be used an unlimited amount of times without much risk (if one performs a psychological backround check, see Experiment-1230-05), there's no real reason to withhold this from the D-class. Besides, this could also be used as a sort of reward for more compliant D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I support you thinking, SCP-294 is in a similar situation. This coffee machine, while dangerous if abused, is kept out in the open, in a break room, and only costs 50 cent to use no less. (I realize that your original post was alllll the way back in 2012, and it may seem unprofessional to comment, but I just wanted to express my enjoyment of the article and my belief that SCP can have some leeway with their containment methods.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good, the part with soaked pages made me cri (not really but)

Only complaining about the

affect beings “with an imagination”

part. Maybe change it to "creative imagination" , since it is known that many animals understand the concept of death?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's nice to see a scip that isn't weird or creepy for a change, even better, a scip that actually tries to be good and make people happy. Original and satisfying, upvoted.

item.title: I like it!
item.content:encoded: Rated this up. I want this book.

item.title: I love it.
item.content:encoded: I love it. The concept, the execution. This definitely goes on my "SCPs that I wish were real" list. Maybe if I had this I wouldn't stay up so late playing video games.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. A bit melodramatic, but one of my personal favourites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Already have it in mind.

D-class wakes up

Dr. ████: D-####, How do you feel? What happened?

D-class: Good sir, my name is Fred. Will you kindly help me out of this bed? I would tell you what happened, though to speak of it fills me with dread. Because, I fear, that I have been… naaauuuughty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: write a cross test with 423. Maybe fred enters the book, no new text appears, but when the next person dreamt under the book's effects, they found in their dream that Fred was halfway through his own dream, Fred was in an argument with the book keeper, Fred acts as the dreamer's sword caddy (or something), or that Fred appears next to the book keeper, sometimes speaking with him, almost as if being his assistant. Later, maybe Fred would go back to own book, and say something like, "Could one of you get a story for me about dreams? I think I'd like to go back."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
Very well done, as well as a very appealing concept, though I must say I feel that this is one of the articles that would most benefit from some background/an explanation of where it might have come from: is this Book Keeper a construct created by a person who made the book? Was he once an author or storyteller who loved his occupation so much that he put himself or a copy of himself into the book? Is it something that just sort of appeared after a while as a physical embodiment of a desired ideal? Is it an old thing hidden away in a library for years untouched, or was it created very recently? Does it only work with English speakers? It says Prof. B raised a family, does that mean eroticism can be involved? If so, to what degree? What would happen if someone read it, but was put in a coma before they next fell asleep, and then stayed there for a year or more?

TL;DR To make clearly upbeat skips fit better with the tone of the site, I think it helps to include more information that you'd expect the Foundation to gather about what it is, how it works, and where it came from (I'd start with an interview, or an attempt at one, with the Bookkeeper trying to figure out what he knows).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of those few SCP's I would really love to get my hands on.
I know they are supposed to creep or gross you out, but every once in a while such an entry is nice to find.
+1ed.
does it do science fiction as well ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. :) A cheery SCP every so often makes things seem more verisimilitudinous (sorry, couldn't think of a better term) since not every anomalous item the Foundation discovered would necessarily be "bad."

Also because the Book Keeper is like the friendliest, kindliest Dungeon Master ever, with his own universe to play with to make his players happy, and that just makes my imagination run.

This SCP could possibly be used as a therapeutic tool for after high-stress assignments, with the proper psychological oversight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my first time commenting on an article, and I have a few things to say:

1) I WANT THAT BOOK! I don't really imagine much people NOT wanting the book, but I still feel like it's worth mentioning.

2) I want to see what would happen if you put this between 826. would the book keeper be able to leave the green book?

3) can the book keeper shape shift in the dream land? someone asking him that question's the only thing I'd want to see added, and I don't think it would add much, if anything.

4) +1, though that might be slightly influenced by thing to say #1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't fully explain why, but I really dislike this one. Maybe it "hits too close to home" for me, but it also seems to be covering too much in terms of realistic mental/emotional problems and giving not enough depth or sense of purpose/gravitas to either of it. The idea of "can't go back, love it here too much, i suicide because i have to leave" seems too patently cheap/false a resolution, and somehow disingenuous, along with the bearded guy's token "boohoo i tried" response

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has to be one of my favorites so far. I haven't come across many SCPs that are actually completely sympathetic, and like Bryx I immediately thought of this one when I read the "happy SCPs are a breath of fresh air" statement.

I do wonder, however, what the effects of repeated exposure would be. Given that "The Book Keeper" genuinely wants to entertain people and make them happy, I would assume that he would allow repeat "visits". Are the psychological effects completely dependent on the user, like with any non-anomalous fantasy medium? Or are there side-effects that begin to present after multiple uses? Moreover, since he appears to have control over whether the trigger phase appears, does "The Book Keeper" have the power to deny access to people he deems unstable, to avoid a repeat of the suicide incident?

Also, based on other articles, I think that rather than classifying both the book and its keeper as SPC-1230, you may want to consider designating one SPC-1230-1 and the other SPC-1230-2. Think of them like a sentient SCP and its habitat. They aren't completely one and the same, especially since "The Book Keeper" interacts with subjects after they've read the key phrase, regardless of distance from the book itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the lengthy review! I'll try and answer a few of your questions to clarify.

As for developing skills, I'd say it's a bit of both. In their dream, they wouldn't suddenly become a master swordsman (unless they really wanted to!) but they'd at least be brought up to a decent skill with a sword, and foes might just so happen to not be blocking at the moment the reader strikes. However, none of the skills would carry over once the reader awakens.

And as for use in training personnel, that actually sounds really interesting and I hadn't thought of it before. It's amusing to picture a labcoat-clad Book Keeper welcoming dreaming personnel to the "D-SCP Foundation", instructing them of the dangers they would face in the actual Foundation.

Lastly, I actually never intended to redact the advice but I was persuaded to do so, to add a bit of mystery to the story. Even today, I'm not sure if I agree with it, but I'm well beyond making such changes to the story. I had meant to imply that once the Book Keeper resumed, he kept the "adventures" brief and made absolutely sure to kick them out after their time was up.
